text: A 10-day heat acclimation program (exercising at ~50% VO2max in 40°C) increases VO2max by 5% in cool conditions and 8% in hot conditions in trained cyclists.
Heat acclimation improves time-trial performance by 6% in cool (13°C) and 8% in hot (38°C) conditions.
Heat acclimation increases power output at lactate threshold by 5% in both cool and hot conditions.
Heat acclimation increases plasma volume by approximately 6.5%, contributing to improved cardiovascular performance.
Heat acclimation increases maximal cardiac output by 9.1% in cool conditions and 4.5% in hot conditions.
Heat acclimation benefits transfer to temperate-cool environments, not just hot conditions, making it a viable training augmentation strategy regardless of race climate.

Equivalent training volume performed in cool conditions (13°C) produces no improvements in VO2max, time-trial performance, lactate threshold, or physiological parameters, confirming heat exposure as the key stimulus.
